name: Science Education 11–12: Curriculum, Pedagogy and Inclusive Practice
week0Message: Have a look around at the first few tiles below — we'll get started next week!

learningOutcomes.title: Science Content & Laboratory Practice
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the concepts, substance and structure of the content and teaching strategies of the 7–10 science syllabus by incorporating practical activities into the laboratory so that students are actively engaged in learning.

learningOutcomes.title: Curriculum Sequencing & Pedagogy
learningOutcomes.description: Organise content into an effective learning and teaching sequence within the context of the 7–10 syllabus in logical, sequential and structured ways utilising appropriate pedagogy to address student learning outcomes.

learningOutcomes.title: Lesson Planning & Conceptual Development
learningOutcomes.description: Plan lesson sequences using knowledge of student learning, content and effective teaching strategies by recognising links in teaching sequences which will develop conceptual understanding and scientific skills and clearly articulate the learning goals.

learningOutcomes.title: Assessment Design & Program Evaluation
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate broad knowledge of strategies that can be used to evaluate teaching programs to improve student learning by planning and documenting an assessment task which includes a range of embedded strategies for assessing student achievement and appropriately mapping outcomes to these strategies.

learningOutcomes.title: Diversity & Responsive Teaching
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of teaching strategies, including literacy and numeracy development, that are responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.

learningOutcomes.title: Professional Learning & Community
learningOutcomes.description: Understand the rationale for continued professional learning, and relevant sources of professional learning including external professionals and community representatives, that can be used to improve personal development and student learning.

supportCallout: Anything going on that may impact your success in this unit? Don't hesitate to reach out to your lecturer sooner rather than later. We're here to support you.

1.title: The K–10 Continuum and Science Education

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE362. This week we begin by stepping back from Stage 6 and examining the full K–10 science continuum — the foundation of prior knowledge and experience your senior students bring with them into Stage 6.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 1 examines how the NSW Science K–10 curriculum connects to the Stage 6 syllabuses. You will trace the progression of key concepts, skills, and Working Scientifically strands from primary through to early secondary science, and consider how understanding this continuum helps you plan more responsive and connected Stage 6 lessons.

1.liveSessionFocus: In our first live session, we introduce the unit, examine the K–10 to Stage 6 transition, and begin mapping how prior learning shapes what students bring to senior science.
liveSessionTasks: Before the session, review the Science K–10 syllabus outcomes for Stage 5 in your primary teaching area and identify two or three concepts that directly underpin Stage 6 content.
Consider: what assumptions do Stage 6 syllabuses make about what students already know from K–10? Where might those assumptions break down?
Come prepared to share one example of a misconception or gap in prior knowledge you have observed (or anticipate) in senior science students.

2.title: The Nature of Science and Science Teaching

announcementBody.intro: This week we turn to the epistemological foundations of science itself — what science is, how it works, and what it means to teach it honestly and rigorously in a senior secondary context.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 2 explores the Nature of Science (NOS) as a professional and pedagogical framework. You will examine the values, practices, and commitments that distinguish science as a way of knowing, and consider how a clear understanding of NOS shapes the way you select content, design investigations, and communicate the purpose of science to students.

2.liveSessionFocus: Session 2 covers Topics 1 and 2 together. We will examine the K–10 continuum and the Nature of Science as connected foundations for planning intellectually honest and engaging senior science lessons.
liveSessionTasks: Review the Nature and Development of Science strand (or equivalent) in your Stage 6 syllabus and identify how NOS concepts are embedded in the content and outcomes.
Identify one aspect of the Nature of Science that you find genuinely challenging to communicate clearly to senior students.
Consider: how does your understanding of NOS influence the way you would design an investigation or respond to a student who says 'just give me the answer'?

3.title: Selecting and Critically Evaluating Resources

announcementBody.intro: This week we develop your skills as a critical consumer and selector of teaching resources — a practical and ongoing professional responsibility that shapes the quality of every lesson you teach.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 3 examines how to evaluate resources systematically and critically across different formats: textbooks, digital tools, laboratory kits, online platforms, and teacher-created materials. You will develop a personal evaluation framework grounded in syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and pedagogical appropriateness, and begin building a curated resource library for your teaching area.

3.liveSessionFocus: Session 3 focuses on resource evaluation frameworks and strategies for building a coherent, high-quality teaching toolkit across the Stage 6 science syllabuses.
liveSessionTasks: Select one textbook or digital resource used in your primary teaching area and evaluate it against your own criteria for quality — consider syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and how it supports student thinking.
Identify one specific strength and one clear weakness in the resource, and consider what you would do to address the weakness in practice.
Come prepared to share your evaluation briefly — we will compare criteria and approaches across different teaching areas in the session.

4.title: Introduction to Teaching Differentiation

announcementBody.intro: This week we turn our attention to one of the most powerful tools in any teacher's repertoire — questioning. Not just asking questions, but designing them deliberately to do different cognitive work. We look at why open questions reveal thinking while closed questions shut it down, how Bloom's Taxonomy and the Q-Matrix can sharpen your questions across different levels of cognitive demand, and what psychological safety has to do with whether students answer at all. The central idea this week, from Eddie Woo: the person doing the talking is the person doing the learning.

4.liveSessionFocus: Designing questions that shift cognitive work to students — from closed recall to open inquiry
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Topic 4 readings and lecture before the live session — pay particular attention to the Koufetta-Menicou & Scaife (2000) classification table of nine question types
Watch the Eddie Woo video and note one phrase or moment that changes how you think about classroom talk

Identify which question types from the Koufetta-Menicou & Scaife classification you currently use most in your teaching or placements, and which you rarely use — bring your reflection to the live session
Complete at least one of the three forum activities before or during the live session
4.orientationNote: Topic 4 sits at the midpoint of the unit and marks a shift — from what you teach to how you teach it. The questioning techniques you develop here will show up in every lesson you ever run. They are directly relevant to Assessment Task 1: the practical activities you design in Part B and the Depth Study in Part C both require deliberate questioning moments — at the toolbox safety briefing, during investigation setup, and in the closing reflection. As you work through the materials, keep asking: what would I actually say to a class of Year 9 students at this moment?
forumPrompts: Choose any practical for any Year group from any source. Identify the key questioning moments in a lesson built around it: (a) What would you ask to open the lesson and activate prior knowledge? (b) What questions would you use in the toolbox safety briefing? (c) What questions would engage students during the investigation itself? (d) What reflective question would close the lesson? Record 3–5 questions with a brief reason for each, then post to the forum.

Using the Q-Matrix or Bloom's Taxonomy as a guide, design a sequence of 4 questions of increasing cognitive demand about a topic from your science discipline. Start at Knowledge/Remembering and work toward at least Analysis or Evaluation level. Post your sequence and explain the progression — why does the order matter?

Locate an image online that could be used as an engagement hook in a Year 7–10 science classroom. Record the URL. Devise 3 questions you would use with students: one to elicit current understanding, one to surface possible misconceptions, one to push higher-order thinking. Post the image URL and your three questions with a brief explanation of each.
4.synthesisTemplate: Thanks everyone for your Topic 4 forum contributions this week. Some strong questioning sequences coming through. A few patterns worth noting across the cohort: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most sequences started well at recall level but the jump to analysis was sometimes abrupt — consider what bridging question sits in between]. One thing to take into the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the distinction between an open question and a genuinely divergent question — open doesn't automatically mean high cognitive demand]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

5.title: The Inclusive Science Classroom

announcementBody.intro: Building on Topic 4, this week we move from the theory of differentiation into the practical challenge of creating genuinely inclusive senior science classrooms — environments where every student can access, engage with, and be challenged by the curriculum.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 5 examines inclusive teaching in the senior science context, with a focus on students with disability, students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds, and students at both ends of the achievement spectrum. You will explore specific adjustments, design principles, and communication strategies, and consider how to maintain academic rigour while broadening access.

5.liveSessionFocus: Session 5 focuses on the practical design of inclusive learning experiences for senior science, including how to adapt tasks, resources, and assessment approaches without reducing the challenge of Stage 6 content.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one adjustment or scaffolding strategy from the topic materials that you could apply immediately to a senior science lesson you are planning or have taught.
Consider how Stage 6 syllabus requirements interact with NESA access arrangements — where do they align well, and where do tensions arise?
Come prepared to discuss a specific scenario: a student with a particular learning need in your senior science class. What would an inclusive response look like?

6.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work

announcementBody.intro: This week we bring together your curriculum knowledge, pedagogical understanding, and resource evaluation skills to focus on the discipline of unit planning — one of the most important and complex professional tasks you will undertake as a science teacher.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 6 examines how to design, sequence, and evaluate a coherent unit of work for Stage 6 science. You will work through the key decisions involved in unit planning: establishing outcomes, selecting and sequencing content, designing assessments for and of learning, choosing resources, and building in opportunities for student reflection and feedback.

6.liveSessionFocus: Session 6 covers Topics 5 and 6 together. We will examine inclusive practice and unit planning as interconnected professional responsibilities, and work through the decisions that shape a coherent, accessible, and outcomes-aligned Stage 6 unit.
liveSessionTasks: Select a topic or module from your Stage 6 syllabus and sketch a rough unit outline — identify the key outcomes, main content sequence, and at least one formative and one summative assessment task.
Evaluate your outline against the unit planning criteria in the topic materials: Is it coherent? Is it outcomes-focused? Does it build in opportunities for feedback?
Come prepared to share your outline and receive peer feedback in the session — bring a willingness to revise.

7.title: Teaching and Learning Science Using Analogies

announcementBody.intro: This week we examine one of the most powerful — and most frequently misused — pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the analogy. Used well, analogies make abstract science concepts concrete and accessible. Used poorly, they embed misconceptions that are hard to shift.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 7 focuses on analogies as tools for conceptual change in science learning. You will examine the theory behind why analogies work, explore frameworks for selecting and presenting analogies effectively (including the Teaching-With-Analogies model), and critically evaluate common analogies used in senior science for their accuracy, accessibility, and potential to mislead.

7.liveSessionFocus: Session 7 examines analogies as pedagogical tools in depth — working through examples from across the senior science syllabuses and practising the skill of evaluating an analogy before using it with students.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one analogy commonly used in your Stage 6 teaching area — write down what concept it is meant to explain, what it maps correctly, and where the mapping breaks down.
Consider how you would introduce this analogy to a class while explicitly addressing its limitations, so students build accurate conceptual understanding rather than a flawed mental model.
Come prepared to present your analogy briefly and receive feedback from peers on how they experience it as learners.

8.title: Developing Your Science Content Knowledge

announcementBody.intro: In our final teaching week, we return to a foundational professional responsibility: the ongoing development of your own science content knowledge. Strong content knowledge is not just a starting point — it is something you actively build and deepen throughout your teaching career.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 8 focuses on content knowledge development as a professional discipline. You will conduct a systematic audit of your content knowledge against your Stage 6 syllabus, identify specific gaps and priorities, and build a realistic plan for ongoing learning — including professional associations, subject-matter resources, and peer networks that support science content development.

8.liveSessionFocus: Session 8 covers Topics 7 and 8 together. We will examine analogies and content knowledge development as complementary professional skills, and conclude the teaching program by looking ahead to the professional learning habits that will sustain your practice.
liveSessionTasks: Complete a content knowledge audit for your primary Stage 6 teaching area: rate your confidence across the major content sections and identify your two or three priority gaps.
For each priority gap, identify at least one concrete resource or action you will take to address it — a specific book, online course, professional association, or peer with relevant expertise.
Come prepared to briefly share your audit findings and your plan — and to give and receive constructive feedback with peers.
